summary: Synarthroses include suture, syndesmosis, and gomphosis which allow very limited motion due to their fixed nature through various connective tissue attachments; thus, any combination thereof satisfies this requirement comprehensively. The answer includes multiple specific examples fitting within one broad category accurately describing a type of articulation characterized by immobility or extremely restricted mobility maintained throughout life typically involving direct bone-to-bone contact without intervening cartilage layers but rather stabilized instead using dense collagenous fibers like sutures between cranial bones among others listed here serving illustrative purposes only across diverse anatomical locations from skull base down into long limb elements where ligaments hold adjacent diaphyses together forming what we know clinically today simply referred to collectively now commonly known broadly speaking just 'synarthrodia'.
